section.title: Judgment Against Samaria and Judah
section.sectionRationale: The book opens like a courtroom summons to the whole earth. The LORD Himself descends from His temple to tread the high places, and the reason is named plainly: the sin of His own people, embodied in their two capitals, Samaria and Jerusalem. The chapter moves from a cosmic theophany to a very local lament, as Micah walks weeping through the towns of his own Judean homeland toward the coming Assyrian storm.
section.thesis: The God who dwells in heaven comes down to judge, because the sin of His covenant people has infected both kingdoms from the capitals outward; yet the prophet who announces the ruin weeps over it, and the judgment is aimed at idolatry, not at the destruction of hope.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the LORD comes down as witness and judge against His people (1-5); the sentence on Samaria the idol city (6-7); and Micah's barefoot lament as calamity rolls through the towns of Judah to the gate of Jerusalem (8-16).

units.label: The LORD comes down to judge
units.note: a summons to all the earth

verses.text: This is the word of the LORD that came to {{micah|Micah}} the Moreshite in the days of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah, what he saw regarding Samaria and Jerusalem:
Hear, O peoples, all of you; listen, O earth, and everyone in it! May the Lord GOD bear witness against you, the Lord from His holy temple.
For behold, the LORD comes forth from His dwelling place; He will come down and tread on the high places of the earth.
The mountains will melt beneath Him, and the valleys will split apart, like wax before the fire, like water rushing down a slope.

All this is for the {{pesha|transgression}} of Jacob and the sins of the house of Israel. What is the transgression of Jacob? Is it not Samaria? And what is the high place of Judah? Is it not Jerusalem?
Therefore I will make Samaria a heap of rubble in the open field, a planting area for a vineyard. I will pour her stones into the valley and expose her foundations.

All her carved images will be smashed to pieces; all her wages will be burned in the fire, and I will destroy all her idols. Since she collected the wages of a prostitute, they will be used again on a prostitute.

units.label: The prophet's lament through the towns

verses.text: Because of this I will lament and wail; I will walk barefoot and naked. I will howl like a jackal and mourn like an ostrich.
For her wound is incurable; it has reached even Judah; it has approached the gate of my people, as far as Jerusalem itself.
Do not tell it in Gath; do not weep at all. Roll in the dust in Beth-leaphrah.
Depart in shameful nakedness, O dwellers of Shaphir. The dwellers of Zaanan will not come out. Beth-ezel is in mourning; its support is taken from you.
For the dwellers of Maroth pined for good, but calamity came down from the LORD, even to the gate of Jerusalem.
Harness your chariot horses, O dweller of Lachish. You were the beginning of sin to the Daughter of Zion, for the transgressions of Israel were found in you.
Therefore, send farewell gifts to Moresheth-gath; the houses of Achzib will prove deceptive to the kings of Israel.
I will again bring a conqueror against you, O dweller of Mareshah. The glory of Israel will come to Adullam.
Shave yourselves bald and cut off your hair in mourning for your precious children; make yourselves as bald as an eagle, for they will go from you into exile.

micah.gloss: The prophet's name is a shortened form of a question, Who is like the LORD? It is a small sermon in a single word. The book that opens with this name closes in 7:18 by answering it: Who is a God like You, who pardons iniquity? The judgment announced here and the mercy promised there both flow from the incomparable God whose name Micah carries.

pesha.gloss: Transgression, rebellion, a willful breach of relationship. It is stronger than a simple mistake; it pictures a deliberate revolt against a rightful lord. Micah traces the coming ruin not to bad luck or mere weakness but to the covenant people's rebellion, which had settled into their very capitals, Samaria and Jerusalem.

basic.context: Micah was a country preacher from Moresheth, a small town in the Judean lowlands, who spoke for God in the same years as Isaiah, roughly 750 to 700 years before Christ. He watched the northern kingdom, with its capital Samaria, fall to Assyria, and he warned that the same judgment was rolling south toward Jerusalem. Chapter one opens like a courtroom: God summons the whole earth, comes down from heaven, and names the charge, the sin of His own people.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real prophecy about real cities, and much of it came true within Micah's lifetime when Assyria devastated the land. The point is not that God is cruel but that sin, especially idolatry, is deadly serious even, and especially, among God's own people. The many town names in verses 10 to 16 are a series of grief-stricken puns, Micah weeping his way through his own neighborhood as the disaster approaches.
basic.originalLanguages: The prophet's very name, Micah, is short for the question Who is like the LORD? The charge against the people is pesha, transgression, a word for deliberate rebellion, not a mere slip. So the God who is beyond compare is met not with worship but with revolt, and that revolt had taken up residence in the two capital cities.
basic.typology: The LORD coming down to tread the high places foreshadows every coming of God in judgment, and ultimately the day when Christ returns to judge the earth. The prophet who weeps over the doomed cities anticipates Jesus weeping over Jerusalem, a judge whose heart breaks over those He must warn.
basic.christ: Micah's tears over a guilty people point straight to Jesus, who wept over Jerusalem and said, how often I have longed to gather your children. The God who comes down here to judge is the same God who will come down in the flesh to save. And the incurable wound of verse 9 finds its only cure at the cross, where the judgment falls on Christ instead of us.
basic.god: God is holy and cannot ignore sin, even in the people He loves. He is also the true King who sees, who summons, who acts, and who is grieved. He is not indifferent to idolatry; He treats it as spiritual adultery. Yet even His judgment has a purpose: to strip away the idols that were destroying His people.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Kings 17 records the fall of Samaria that Micah foresaw. Jeremiah 26 remembers how Micah's preaching moved King Hezekiah to repentance. Luke 19 shows Jesus weeping over Jerusalem as Micah wept over Judah. Amos 4 pictures the same God treading the high places of the earth.
basic.oneStory: The book of Micah begins where the whole Bible often begins, with a holy God confronting the sin of His people. But the tears of the prophet already hint that judgment is not the last word. The God who is like no other, whose name Micah carries, will make a way to pardon the very rebellion He here condemns, a way that runs through Bethlehem and the cross.

context.literary: The chapter is a masterfully constructed opening. It begins with a universal summons and a theophany, God descending in earth-melting majesty, then narrows swiftly to the specific sin of Samaria, and finally to a deeply personal lament in which Micah walks through a dozen towns of his own region. The town names in verses 10 to 16 form a chain of Hebrew wordplays, each town's name sounding like the disaster that befalls it, so that the geography itself preaches.
context.historical: Micah of Moresheth-gath ministered in Judah under Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, contemporary with Isaiah in Jerusalem and shortly after Amos and Hosea in the north. He lived through the Assyrian conquest of Samaria in 722 BC and the invasion of Judah by Sennacherib in 701 BC, which devastated the very Shephelah towns he names, including Lachish and his own Moresheth. His was a rural voice warning the corridors of power.
context.canonical: As the sixth of the Book of the Twelve, Micah stands alongside Isaiah as an eighth-century witness. Its opening theophany joins a stream of texts, from Sinai to the Psalms to Amos, where God comes down in fire and quaking to judge. Jeremiah 26:18 explicitly cites Micah 3:12, showing that later generations remembered Micah as a prophet whose word both threatened and, through repentance, spared Jerusalem.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce, as an eyewitness of God's counsel, that the LORD is coming to judge the covenant people for their rebellion, centered in the idolatry of their capitals, and to move the hearers to grief and repentance before the blow falls.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical judgment on Samaria and Judah; it does not prescribe that believers seek such ruin. The abiding truths are the holiness of God, the deadly seriousness of idolatry among God's people, and the fitting response of lament and repentance.

debates.question: Are the coming of the LORD and the melting mountains in verses 3 to 4 literal or figurative?

views.label: Poetic theophany language
views.case: The imagery draws on the standard vocabulary of God appearing at Sinai and in the Psalms; melting mountains and splitting valleys are vivid poetry for the overwhelming approach of the Judge, not a geological forecast.

views.label: Real historical intervention
views.case: The poetry describes an actual coming of God in the historical judgments of Assyria against Samaria and Judah, and points forward to the final day when God truly shakes the earth.

debates.bearing: Either way the point is the same: the living God really acts in history to judge sin, and no created power can stand before Him when He comes.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is framed by the prophet's own name. Micah is a compressed question, Who is like the LORD, mi-kayahu, and it silently indicts a people who treat the incomparable God as if He were one idol among many. The charge is pesha, transgression, a term for deliberate rebellion against a rightful sovereign, which Micah locates precisely, not in vague human frailty but in Samaria and Jerusalem, the political and religious hearts of the two kingdoms. Then the lament in verses 10 to 16 turns on a cascade of puns: Gath sounds like tell, Beth-leaphrah like house of dust, Shaphir means pleasant, Zaanan sounds like come out, Maroth like bitter, Lachish like team of horses, Achzib means deception, and Mareshah sounds like conqueror. The prophet's grief is so deep that the very names of his home towns become a dirge, each syllable dripping with the ruin about to come.

typology.type: The LORD coming down from His temple to tread the high places
typology.fulfillment: The comings of God in judgment throughout Scripture, consummated in the return of Christ to judge the living and the dead, Revelation 19.

typology.type: The weeping prophet walking barefoot through doomed towns
typology.fulfillment: Jesus weeping over Jerusalem, the compassionate Judge grieved by the ruin sin brings, Luke 19:41-44.

typology.type: The incurable wound reaching to the gate of Jerusalem
typology.fulfillment: The sin of the world that only the cross can heal, where the true wound is borne by Christ, Isaiah 53:5.

study.christ: Micah 1 shows the God who comes down to judge, and the New Testament shows that same God coming down in the flesh, not first to judge but to save. The tears Micah sheds over his doomed homeland are answered in the tears of Jesus over Jerusalem, longing to gather her children under His wings. The wound Micah calls incurable, spreading from Samaria to the very gate of Jerusalem, is the wound of sin that no reform can heal; it finds its only cure at Golgotha, where the judgment that fell on the cities in Micah's day falls instead on the sinless Son. The God who is like no other, whose name Micah bears, is fully revealed in Christ, in whom holiness and mercy meet.
study.god: This chapter reveals God as the holy King who will not treat sin lightly, least of all among His own covenant people. He is the witness against them from His holy temple, the Judge who comes down in majesty, and the wronged husband who calls their idolatry the wages of a prostitute. Yet He is not cold: the lament that fills the chapter is His prophet's grief, and behind it His own. He judges idolatry precisely because it was destroying the people He loved, and even the exile He announces is aimed at cleansing, not annihilation.

crossReferences.note: The fall of Samaria to Assyria in 722 BC, the judgment Micah announces in verses 6 to 7.
Elders recall Micah's preaching, which led Hezekiah to seek the LORD, sparing Jerusalem in that generation.
Micah's contemporary opens with the same courtroom summons, Hear, O heavens, for the LORD has spoken.
Jesus weeps over Jerusalem as Micah wept over the doomed towns of Judah.

study.oneStory: Micah opens the way the whole biblical drama often opens: a holy God confronting the rebellion of His own people, coming down in majesty to judge. But the prophet's tears, and the very meaning of his name, already whisper that mercy is coming. The incurable wound of sin that here reaches to the gate of Jerusalem will one day be healed, not by reform but by a ruler born in a nearby town, Bethlehem, who bears the judgment Himself. Chapter one sets the problem the rest of the book, and the rest of the Bible, will answer in Christ.

sermon.why: No focused expository sermon on Micah 1 by a trusted expositor could be verified by web search this session, so none is cited rather than risk misattribution.

quiz.q: Why is the LORD coming down to judge, according to verse 5?

options.text: Because of the transgression of Jacob and the sins of Israel, centered in Samaria and Jerusalem.
options.note: Verse 5 names the people's rebellion, lodged in the two capitals, as the cause.

options.text: Because the surrounding pagan nations had attacked Israel.
options.note: Foreign invasion was the means God later used, but verse 5 names His own people's sin as the reason He comes.

options.text: Because the land had suffered a natural disaster.
options.note: The melting mountains are poetry for God's coming; the cause is sin, not nature.

options.text: Because God is arbitrary and simply wished to destroy.
options.note: The text gives a clear moral cause and shows God's grief; He is not arbitrary.

quiz.q: What does the prophet's name, Micah, mean, and why does it matter here?

options.text: Who is like the LORD, underlining that the incomparable God is met with idolatry and rebellion.
options.note: The name frames the book and is answered in 7:18, Who is a God like You who pardons.

options.text: The LORD saves, pointing to deliverance from Assyria.
options.note: That is the meaning of Joshua or Jesus, not Micah.

options.text: Servant of the LORD, stressing Micah's humble role.
options.note: Micah is a servant, but his name specifically asks who is like the LORD.

options.text: Comfort, signaling the book is only about hope.
options.note: The name is a question about God's incomparability, and the book holds both judgment and hope.

quiz.q: How does Micah respond to the judgment he must announce (verse 8)?

options.text: He laments and wails, walking barefoot, grieving over his people.
options.note: Verse 8 shows the prophet weeping, not gloating, over the coming ruin.

options.text: He rejoices that his enemies will finally be punished.
options.note: Micah grieves; there is no gloating over the doom of his people.

options.text: He stays silent and detached from the message.
options.note: He is anything but detached; he mourns openly and dramatically.

options.text: He demands that God relent immediately.
options.note: He grieves and pleads over the coming ruin, though verse 8 shows open lament rather than an explicit demand that God relent.

quiz.q: What is the point of the many town names in verses 10 to 16?

options.text: They are grief-filled wordplays as Micah weeps through his own homeland toward the coming disaster.
options.note: Each town's name is punned with the ruin that befalls it, a geography of lament.

options.text: They list the towns that escaped the judgment untouched.
options.note: The towns are marked for calamity, not spared; the section is a dirge.

options.text: They simply record boundaries for later land division.
options.note: These are real towns of Micah's homeland, but the passage laments their coming ruin rather than recording land boundaries.

options.text: They name the birthplaces of future kings.
options.note: The section is about coming ruin, not royal genealogy.

quiz.q: How does this chapter point forward to Christ?

options.text: The God who comes down to judge, and the prophet who weeps over the city, are fulfilled in Jesus, who wept over Jerusalem and bore the wound of sin.
options.note: Micah's judging God and weeping prophet both find their answer in Christ.

options.text: It predicts the exact date of Christ's birth.
options.note: Micah does point toward Christ's birthplace, though that comes in chapter 5 and no date is given; chapter 1 points to Christ more broadly.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ at all.
options.note: The judging, grieving God of Micah 1 is fully revealed in Jesus.

options.text: It teaches that judgment is the final word with no hope.
options.note: The prophet's tears and his name already point beyond judgment to mercy.

questions: Micah names sin plainly, even in God's own people. Where might you be tolerating an idol or a rebellion that you have stopped calling by its true name?
The prophet wept over the judgment he announced. When you think about God's judgment on sin, does it move you to grief and prayer for others, or to indifference or even satisfaction?
God called the people's idolatry spiritual adultery. What competes with God for your deepest loyalty and affection right now?
The wound is called incurable, yet the whole book moves toward the God who pardons. How does it change your view of a besetting sin to know that only Christ, not self-reform, can heal it?
Micah's name asks, Who is like the LORD? Spend a moment answering that question about God's holiness and His mercy. How does holding both together shape the way you come to Him?

translationNotes.note: Moreshite marks Micah as a man of Moresheth-gath, a town in the Judean lowlands, a rural prophet addressing the capitals of both kingdoms.
The creatures rendered jackal and ostrich are uncertain; the BSB footnote offers serpent or dragon, and literally daughters of an ostrich or owl. The sense is a mournful, eerie howling of grief.

This section is built on Hebrew puns tying each town's name to its fate, as the BSB footnotes show: Gath sounds like tell, Beth-leaphrah means house of dust, Shaphir means pleasant, Zaanan sounds like come out, Maroth like bitter, Lachish like team of horses, Achzib means deception, and Mareshah sounds like conqueror.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Micah 1:1-16. All 16 verses reproduced contiguously. The em dash in verse 1 (kings of Judah, what he saw) was rendered as a comma to keep the Makor no-dash style while preserving the wording.
